main_paper.raw: {"methods":["Predictive coding networks (PCNs) with sparse and non-negative constraints to model grid cell emergence from place cell representations","Temporal predictive coding network (tPCN), a dynamical extension of PCNs, trained on path integration tasks","Analytical comparison of tPCN Hebbian learning rule with truncated backpropagation through time (BPTT)"],"problems":["How grid cells in the medial entorhinal cortex (MEC) are learned in a biologically plausible manner","Lack of biologically plausible learning rules for the emergence of grid cells in neural circuits","Extending predictive coding theory beyond visual representations to spatial representations"],"datasets":["Simulated spatial environments for static and dynamic (path integration) tasks"],"metrics":["Emergence of hexagonal grid-like firing patterns in latent representations","Robustness of grid cell emergence under different architectural choices","Ability to develop grid cells without velocity inputs"],"results":[{"metric":"Emergence of hexagonal grid cells in PCNs and tPCNs","value":"Observed"},{"metric":"Robustness of grid cell emergence under different architectures and input conditions","value":"Demonstrated"},{"metric":"Analytical equivalence of tPCN Hebbian learning to truncated BPTT","value":"Shown"}],"novelty_claims":["First demonstration that grid cells naturally emerge in predictive coding networks (PCNs) trained with biologically plausible plasticity rules","Extension of predictive coding theory from visual to spatial (MEC) representations","Analytical demonstration that tPCN's Hebbian learning rule approximates truncated BPTT, providing a normative link between biological and artificial learning rules","Demonstration that grid cells can emerge robustly in tPCN even without velocity inputs, and under various architectural choices"]}

methods: Predictive coding networks (PCNs) with sparse and non-negative constraints to model grid cell emergence from place cell representations
Temporal predictive coding network (tPCN), a dynamical extension of PCNs, trained on path integration tasks
Analytical comparison of tPCN Hebbian learning rule with truncated backpropagation through time (BPTT)
problems: How grid cells in the medial entorhinal cortex (MEC) are learned in a biologically plausible manner
Lack of biologically plausible learning rules for the emergence of grid cells in neural circuits
Extending predictive coding theory beyond visual representations to spatial representations
datasets: Simulated spatial environments for static and dynamic (path integration) tasks
metrics: Emergence of hexagonal grid-like firing patterns in latent representations
Robustness of grid cell emergence under different architectural choices
Ability to develop grid cells without velocity inputs

results.metric: Emergence of hexagonal grid cells in PCNs and tPCNs
results.value: Observed

results.metric: Robustness of grid cell emergence under different architectures and input conditions
results.value: Demonstrated

results.metric: Analytical equivalence of tPCN Hebbian learning to truncated BPTT
results.value: Shown

novelty_claims: First demonstration that grid cells naturally emerge in predictive coding networks (PCNs) trained with biologically plausible plasticity rules
Extension of predictive coding theory from visual to spatial (MEC) representations
Analytical demonstration that tPCN's Hebbian learning rule approximates truncated BPTT, providing a normative link between biological and artificial learning rules
Demonstration that grid cells can emerge robustly in tPCN even without velocity inputs, and under various architectural choices

cycles)","Local Hebbian-style learning rules for synaptic plasticity","Multi-compartment neuron modeling (basal and apical dendrites)","Incorporation of conjunctive velocity inputs for path integration"],"problems":["How the hippocampal formation can learn and use a generative model for sensory and spatial memory in a biologically plausible way","How to unify generative modeling of ability","value":"Model learns to path integrate by developing a ring attractor connectivity structure, matching theoretical and empirical results (no numeric value provided)"},{"metric":"Transfer learning of path integration","value":"Path integration capabilities recover in new environments without needing to relearn weights (qualitative claim)"}],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of a to gate information flow and implement a wake-sleep-like learning regime in a biologically realistic manner.","All synaptic plasticity is local and based on prediction errors between dendritic compartments and soma, unlike previous models requiring non-local learning rules or backpropagation.","The model can learn to path integrate and transfer this ability between environments without methods: Biologically plausible generative model of the hippocampal formation (Helmholtz machine architecture)
Theta-band oscillation-gated information flow (wake-sleep cycles)
Local Hebbian-style learning rules for synaptic plasticity
Multi-compartment neuron modeling (basal and apical dendrites)
Incorporation of conjunctive velocity inputs for path integration
problems: How the hippocampal formation can learn and use a generative model for sensory and spatial memory in a biologically plausible way
How to unify generative modeling of sensory stimuli and path integration in a single biologically plausible framework
How to enable transfer of learned path integration structure between environments without relearning
datasets: Simulated spatial exploration task (agent moving on a 1D track)
Synthetic high-dimensional sensory input streams
metrics: Accuracy of latent state inference
Quality of sensory predictions (realism)
Ability to path integrate (maintenance of position estimate in absence of sensory cues)
Generalization/transfer of path integration to new environments

results.value: Model accurately infers the latent state of high-dimensional sensory environments (quantitative values not specified in provided text)
Model generates realistic sensory predictions (qualitative claim, no numeric value provided)
Model learns to path integrate by developing a ring attractor connectivity structure, matching theoretical and empirical results (no numeric value provided)
Path integration capabilities recover in new environments without needing to relearn weights (qualitative claim)

novelty_claims: Introduction of a biologically plausible generative model of the hippocampal formation trained with theta-driven local learning rules, unifying generative modeling and path integration.
Use of theta-band oscillations to gate information flow and implement a wake-sleep-like learning regime in a biologically realistic manner.
All synaptic plasticity is local and based on prediction errors between dendritic compartments and soma, unlike previous models requiring non-local learning rules or backpropagation.
The model can learn to path integrate and transfer this ability between environments without relearning, demonstrating a form of transfer learning not present in previous models.
Extension and simplification of previous theoretical models to a temporally continuous, spatial navigation task with learned, not fixed, connectivity.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Training biologically plausible neural networks to perform path integration","Analyzing connectome and activity maps of trained networks to elucidate mechanisms","Developing a unifying theoretical framework for the emergence of hexagonal grid patterns"],"problems":["Understanding the mechanistic origins of hexagonal grid cell firing fields","Explaining the computational significance of grid cells in path integration","Accounting for biological variability in grid cell models"],"datasets":[],"metrics":[],"results":[],"novelty_claims":["Demonstrates that hexagonal grid patterns arise generically in neural networks trained for path integration, linking computational and mechanistic origins.","Proposes a unifying theory explaining the ubiquity of hexagonal grids in path-integrator circuits.","Introduces methods to analyze connectome and activity maps of trained networks, providing mechanistic insights and a roadmap for interpreting connectomic and physiological data.","Shows that trained networks exhibit realistic biological variability not captured by hand-designed models."]}

methods: Training biologically plausible neural networks to perform path integration
Analyzing connectome and activity maps of trained networks to elucidate mechanisms
Developing a unifying theoretical framework for the emergence of hexagonal grid patterns
problems: Understanding the mechanistic origins of hexagonal grid cell firing fields
Explaining the computational significance of grid cells in path integration
Accounting for biological variability in grid cell models
novelty_claims: Demonstrates that hexagonal grid patterns arise generically in neural networks trained for path integration, linking computational and mechanistic origins.
Proposes a unifying theory explaining the ubiquity of hexagonal grids in path-integrator circuits.
Introduces methods to analyze connectome and activity maps of trained networks, providing mechanistic insights and a roadmap for interpreting connectomic and physiological data.
Shows that trained networks exhibit realistic biological variability not captured by hand-designed models.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Non-negative sparse coding model for learning spatial representations from grid cell inputs","Unified learning model to describe both hippocampal place cells and dentate gyrus granule cells"],"problems":["How place cells and granule cells with different spatial tuning properties can emerge from grid cell inputs","Understanding the role of sparse coding in the formation of spatial representations in the hippocampus"],"datasets":["Simulated grid cell activity with various orientations, spacings, and phases","Simulated weakly-tuned MEC cell activity"],"metrics":["Efficiency of spatial tiling","Number of firing locations per model cell"],"results":[{"metric":"Efficiency of spatial tiling","value":"Model cells with diverse grid cell inputs efficiently tile the entire spatial environment with single-location fields"},{"metric":"Number of firing locations per model cell","value":"Reduced diversity in grid cell parameters or fewer cells in the network leads to model cells with multiple firing locations"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a unified non-negative sparse coding model that accounts for both place cell and granule cell spatial tuning properties","Demonstrates that diversity in grid cell parameters is necessary for single-location place fields to emerge","Shows that place cell-like representations can emerge even from weakly-tuned MEC cells using non-negative sparse coding"]}

methods: Non-negative sparse coding model for learning spatial representations from grid cell inputs
Unified learning model to describe both hippocampal place cells and dentate gyrus granule cells
problems: How place cells and granule cells with different spatial tuning properties can emerge from grid cell inputs
Understanding the role of sparse coding in the formation of spatial representations in the hippocampus
datasets: Simulated grid cell activity with various orientations, spacings, and phases
Simulated weakly-tuned MEC cell activity
metrics: Efficiency of spatial tiling
Number of firing locations per model cell

results.value: Model cells with diverse grid cell inputs efficiently tile the entire spatial environment with single-location fields

results.metric: Number of firing locations per model cell
results.value: Reduced diversity in grid cell parameters or fewer cells in the network leads to model cells with multiple firing locations

novelty_claims: Proposes a unified non-negative sparse coding model that accounts for both place cell and granule cell spatial tuning properties
Demonstrates that diversity in grid cell parameters is necessary for single-location place fields to emerge
Shows that place cell-like representations can emerge even from weakly-tuned MEC cells using non-negative sparse coding

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["High-density neuronal recordings in the medial entorhinal cortex (MEC) and hippocampal area CA1 of rats during goal-directed behavior in an open field","Analysis of grid cell firing patterns to identify predictive grid cells","Phase analysis of grid cell firing relative to hippocampal CA1 theta oscillations"],"problems":["Does the medial entorhinal cortex contribute to the prediction of an animal’s future location during spatial navigation?","How do grid cells in the MEC represent prospective spatial information during goal-directed behavior?"],"datasets":["Neuronal recordings from rats performing goal-directed behavior in an open field"],"metrics":["Grid field shift relative to direction of travel","Phase of grid cell firing relative to hippocampal CA1 theta oscillation"],"results":[{"metric":"Grid field shift against direction of travel","value":"Predictive grid cells showed grid fields shifted ahead of the animal's current position, representing future locations"},{"metric":"Theta phase of predictive grid cell firing","value":"Predictive grid cells discharged at the trough phases of the hippocampal CA1 theta oscillation"}],"novelty_claims":["Discovery of predictive grid cells in the medial entorhinal cortex that represent future, not current, locations during goal-directed behavior","Demonstration that predictive grid cells organize sequences of trajectory from current to future positions across each theta cycle","Evidence that the medial entorhinal cortex provides a predictive map supporting forward planning in spatial navigation"]}

methods: High-density neuronal recordings in the medial entorhinal cortex (MEC) and hippocampal area CA1 of rats during goal-directed behavior in an open field
Analysis of grid cell firing patterns to identify predictive grid cells
Phase analysis of grid cell firing relative to hippocampal CA1 theta oscillations
problems: Does the medial entorhinal cortex contribute to the prediction of an animal’s future location during spatial navigation?

datasets: Neuronal recordings from rats performing goal-directed behavior in an open field
metrics: Grid field shift relative to direction of travel
Phase of grid cell firing relative to hippocampal CA1 theta oscillation

results.metric: Grid field shift against direction of travel
results.value: Predictive grid cells showed grid fields shifted ahead of the animal's current position, representing future locations

results.metric: Theta phase of predictive grid cell firing
results.value: Predictive grid cells discharged at the trough phases of the hippocampal CA1 theta oscillation

novelty_claims: Discovery of predictive grid cells in the medial entorhinal cortex that represent future, not current, locations during goal-directed behavior
Demonstration that predictive grid cells organize sequences of trajectory from current to future positions across each theta cycle
Evidence that the medial entorhinal cortex provides a predictive map supporting forward planning in spatial navigation

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Training recurrent neural networks (RNNs) to perform spatial navigation tasks in 2D arenas using velocity (speed and heading direction) inputs","Applying biologically relevant constraints during RNN training"],"problems":["Understanding the mechanisms and functional significance of grid-like and other spatial representations in the Entorhinal Cortex (EC)","Explaining the emergence of diverse spatially tuned neural responses (grid cells, border cells, band-like cells) in EC","Developing a unified model that can account for multiple types of spatial responses observed in EC, not just grid cells"],"datasets":["Simulated 2D navigation environments (arenas) for training and evaluating RNNs"],"metrics":["Accuracy of RNN output in representing the animal's physical location"],"results":[{"metric":"Location tracking accuracy","value":"RNN output can accurately, though not perfectly, track the animal’s location during navigation"}],"novelty_claims":["First demonstration that grid-like responses can emerge from training a recurrent neural network to perform navigation tasks","The trained RNN naturally gives rise to a variety of spatial response profiles observed in EC, including grid-like, border, and band-like cells, without requiring fine-tuned connectivity","The order of emergence of grid-like and border cells in the model is consistent with developmental observations in biological systems","Provides a unified model that can simultaneously explain different types of neural responses in EC"]}

methods: Training recurrent neural networks (RNNs) to perform spatial navigation tasks in 2D arenas using velocity (speed and heading direction) inputs
Applying biologically relevant constraints during RNN training
problems: Understanding the mechanisms and functional significance of grid-like and other spatial representations in the Entorhinal Cortex (EC)
Explaining the emergence of diverse spatially tuned neural responses (grid cells, border cells, band-like cells) in EC
Developing a unified model that can account for multiple types of spatial responses observed in EC, not just grid cells
datasets: Simulated 2D navigation environments (arenas) for training and evaluating RNNs
metrics: Accuracy of RNN output in representing the animal's physical location

results.value: RNN output can accurately, though not perfectly, track the animal’s location during navigation

novelty_claims: First demonstration that grid-like responses can emerge from training a recurrent neural network to perform navigation tasks
The trained RNN naturally gives rise to a variety of spatial response profiles observed in EC, including grid-like, border, and band-like cells, without requiring fine-tuned connectivity
The order of emergence of grid-like and border cells in the model is consistent with developmental observations in biological systems
Provides a unified model that can simultaneously explain different types of neural responses in EC

methods: Self-supervised learning (SSL) framework for spatial representations in deep recurrent neural networks
Design of data, data augmentations, loss functions, and network architecture motivated by normative principles
Ablation studies to test emergence of grid cells under different constraints
problems: Understanding why the mammalian brain develops multi-modular grid cell representations for space
Synthesizing core principles that lead to the emergence of multi-modular grid cells in deep recurrent neural networks
Developing a learning framework where grid cells emerge without hand-engineered or supervised signals
datasets: Simulated spatial navigation data (details not specified in the provided text)
metrics: Emergence of multi-modular grid cells (qualitative/structural metric)
Generalization of learned representations outside training distribution

results.value: Observed in networks trained with the proposed SSL framework
Networks and representations generalize well

novelty_claims: Proposes a self-supervised learning framework that does not require supervised position information or hand-engineered readout representations for the emergence of grid cells
Combines insights from coding theory, dynamical systems, function optimization, and supervised deep learning into a unified approach
Demonstrates that multi-modular grid cells can emerge as optimal solutions in deep recurrent neural networks trained with the proposed SSL framework
Provides a minimal, theoretically-motivated, and developmentally plausible learning problem for path-integrating deep recurrent networks

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Population autocorrelation: summing 2D autocorrelations of individual grid cells within a module to analyze population-level spatial patterns","Application of population autocorrelation to both simulated and experimental data","Analysis of spatial stability by computing population autocorrelation on the linearized track"],"problems":["Determining whether grid cell firing patterns on a circular track maintain an underlying hexagonal lattice structure","Overcoming limitations of individual grid cell analysis in undersampled environments (such as circular tracks)","Clarifying the interpretation of grid cell activity on a circular track in the context of attractor network theory"],"datasets":["Grid cell recordings from rats traversing a circular track (Jacob et al., 2019)","Simulated grid cell data"],"metrics":["Population autocorrelation","Spatial stability across laps","Lattice spacing and orientation inference"],"results":[{"metric":"Population autocorrelation hexagonal pattern recovery","value":"Underlying six-peak hexagonal pattern is recovered at the population level, not observable in individual cell autocorrelations"},{"metric":"Allocentric code stability","value":"Population autocorrelation on the linearized track shows grid cells maintain an allocentric code for space"},{"metric":"Lattice parameter inference","value":"Spacing and orientation of the population lattice can be inferred and shown to differ across environments"}],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of a novel population autocorrelation approach that pools responses from all grid cells within a module to reveal underlying spatial patterns in undersampled environments","Demonstration that the population-level analysis can recover the hexagonal lattice structure of grid cell firing on a circular track, which is not apparent from individual cell analysis","Ability of the method to infer lattice spacing and orientation in environments where individual cell data is insufficient or noisy","Support for the attractor network theory of grid cells using this new analytical approach"]}

methods: Population autocorrelation: summing 2D autocorrelations of individual grid cells within a module to analyze population-level spatial patterns
Application of population autocorrelation to both simulated and experimental data
Analysis of spatial stability by computing population autocorrelation on the linearized track
problems: Determining whether grid cell firing patterns on a circular track maintain an underlying hexagonal lattice structure
Overcoming limitations of individual grid cell analysis in undersampled environments (such as circular tracks)
Clarifying the interpretation of grid cell activity on a circular track in the context of attractor network theory
datasets: Grid cell recordings from rats traversing a circular track (Jacob et al., 2019)
Simulated grid cell data
metrics: Population autocorrelation
Spatial stability across laps
Lattice spacing and orientation inference

results.value: Underlying six-peak hexagonal pattern is recovered at the population level, not observable in individual cell autocorrelations
Population autocorrelation on the linearized track shows grid cells maintain an allocentric code for space
Spacing and orientation of the population lattice can be inferred and shown to differ across environments

novelty_claims: Introduction of a novel population autocorrelation approach that pools responses from all grid cells within a module to reveal underlying spatial patterns in undersampled environments
Demonstration that the population-level analysis can recover the hexagonal lattice structure of grid cell firing on a circular track, which is not apparent from individual cell analysis
Ability of the method to infer lattice spacing and orientation in environments where individual cell data is insufficient or noisy
Support for the attractor network theory of grid cells using this new analytical approach

methods: Successor Representation (SR) modeling of hippocampal place cells and entorhinal grid cells
Temporal Difference (TD) learning for SR estimation
Analytical computation of SR from transition matrices
Spectral regularization (eigendecomposition) of SR for noise suppression and multiscale structure extraction
Simulation of spatial and non-spatial tasks/environments (e.g., 1D tracks, 2D mazes, multi-compartment environments, abstract graphs)
Comparison of SR-based models to Gaussian and topological place field models
Subgoal discovery using SR eigenvectors and spectral graph theory (normalized cuts)
Simulation of grid cell firing via SR eigenvectors
Quantitative analysis of place and grid field properties (e.g., center of mass, spatial correlation, autocorrelation)
problems: What is the computational function of hippocampal place cells and grid cells beyond spatial mapping?
How does the hippocampus support flexible, efficient prediction and planning in reinforcement learning tasks?
How can neural representations support rapid value recomputation when rewards or policies change?
How do environmental topology, obstacles, and behavioral policy shape hippocampal and entorhinal representations?
How can subgoals for hierarchical planning be discovered from neural representations?
How can noise in neural representations be suppressed while preserving task-relevant structure?
datasets: Simulated spatial environments (1D tracks, 2D rooms with/without obstacles, Tolman detour maze, annular watermaze, hairpin maze, multi-compartment environments)
Simulated non-spatial abstract graph (as in Schapiro et al. 2013)
Empirical data from published neuroscience experiments (e.g., place cell and grid cell recordings, fMRI studies) used for qualitative and quantitative comparison
metrics: Spatial correlation (Fisher z-transformed) between place/grid fields
Center of mass (CoM) of place fields
Grid field orientation and regularity (autocorrelation, ellipse fitting)
Similarity between grid fields in different compartments (Pearson correlation)
Representational similarity (pattern similarity) in hippocampus (fMRI)
Model fit (correlation between simulated and empirical fields)
Within-community vs. between-community SR state similarity

results.metric: Spatial correlation (Fisher z) between place fields near and far from barriers (Tolman detour maze)
results.value: Place fields near barriers change significantly more (lower correlation) than those far from barriers, matching experimental data (Fig. 5C,F)

results.metric: Backward skewing of SR place fields on 1D track with directional bias
results.value: SR model reproduces backward skewing of place fields observed in experiments (Fig. 4)

results.metric: Distribution of SR place fields in annular watermaze with reward
results.value: SR model predicts increased firing near rewarded locations and preceding states, matching empirical findings (Fig. 6)

results.metric: Within-community vs. between-community SR state similarity (abstract graph)
results.value: Within-community similarity is higher, matching hippocampal pattern similarity in fMRI (Fig. 7G)

results.metric: Grid field orientation consistency (square vs. circular environments)
results.value: SR eigenvectors show consistent orientation in square, variable in circular, matching grid cell data (Fig. 9G,J)

results.metric: Similarity between grid fields in two compartments (multi-compartment environment)
results.value: SR model reproduces decline in inter-compartment similarity and grid regularity over training, matching experimental data (Fig. 11C,I)

results.metric: Model fit (correlation) between simulated and empirical grid fields
results.value: SR eigenvector model achieves high correlation with empirical grid fields, especially as training progresses (Fig. 11J)
Identified subgoals (bottleneck states) correspond to doorways and decision points, matching behavioral data (Fig. S14)

novelty_claims: Proposes that the hippocampus encodes a predictive map in the form of the successor representation (SR), rather than a purely spatial cognitive map.
Demonstrates that SR-based representations account for a wide range of empirical findings about place and grid cells, including effects of obstacles, policy, and reward.
Argues that entorhinal grid cells encode a low-dimensional eigendecomposition of the SR, supporting noise suppression and multiscale planning.
Shows that SR eigenvectors can be used for subgoal discovery, providing a normative account of hierarchical planning in the brain.
Introduces spectral regularization as a biologically plausible mechanism for stabilizing predictive maps and improving learning efficiency.
Unifies spatial and non-spatial hippocampal coding under a single predictive framework, explaining pattern similarity in both domains.
Provides a computational model that bridges model-free and model-based reinforcement learning via the SR, enabling rapid value recomputation when rewards change.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Explicit covariance-learning predictive coding network (explicit covPCN)","Implicit covariance-learning predictive coding network (implicit covPCN)","Dendritic covariance-learning predictive coding network (dendritic covPCN)","Hybrid predictive coding network (combining implicit covPCN with hierarchical PCN)"],"problems":["Unifying predictive coding and covariance learning in models of associative memory in the hippocampus","Developing biologically plausible and computationally stable recurrent predictive coding models for associative methods: Explicit covariance-learning predictive coding network (explicit covPCN)
Implicit covariance-learning predictive coding network (implicit covPCN)
Dendritic covariance-learning predictive coding network (dendritic covPCN)
Hybrid predictive coding network (combining implicit covPCN with hierarchical PCN)
problems: Unifying predictive coding and covariance learning in models of associative memory in the hippocampus
Developing biologically plausible and computationally stable recurrent predictive coding models for associative memory
Addressing the lack of recurrent structure in hierarchical predictive coding models for associative memory
Overcoming numerical instability and non-local plasticity in explicit covariance-learning predictive coding models
datasets: Not specified in the provided content (mentions use of large structured data such as natural scenes, but no specific datasets named)
metrics: Stability of convergence (computational stability)
Biological plausibility (local computation, local plasticity, architectural similarity)
Pattern completion accuracy (associative memory task performance)
novelty_claims: Proposes a family of recurrent predictive coding networks (covPCNs) that learn covariance information implicitly and plausibly, addressing both computational stability and biological plausibility.
Introduces the implicit covPCN and dendritic covPCN, which use local Hebbian plasticity and can stably perform associative memory tasks, unlike previous explicit covPCN models.

Demonstrates theoretical and empirical equivalence between implicit/dendritic covPCN and explicit covPCN in associative memory tasks, while overcoming the latter's instability and non-local learning rule.

Shows that the new models can be combined with hierarchical predictive coding networks to model hippocampo-neocortical interactions, providing a unified and biologically plausible computational mechanism for associative memory.
Provides a reparameterization of prediction errors in explicit covPCN, leading to simplified free energy forms and more robust, biologically plausible implementations.

problems: Understanding the circuit mechanisms underlying the diversity of spatial tuning in hippocampal and adjacent brain area neurons

novelty_claims: Proposes that all observed spatial tuning patterns (selectivity and invariance) can be explained by the same mechanism: combined excitatory and inhibitory synaptic plasticity driven by input statistics

methods: Temporal Predictive Coding (tPC): a family of predictive coding models extended to handle sequential memory tasks with a biologically plausible neural implementation
Analytical mapping of single-layer tPC to Asymmetric Hopfield Network (AHN) with implicit statistical whitening

results.value: tPC shows more stable performance than AHN and its modern variants in sequential memory tasks with structured inputs
tPC successfully reproduces human-like effects such as the impact of sequence length and the primacy/recency effect

novelty_claims: Proposes temporal predictive coding (tPC), a novel biologically plausible model for sequential memory based on predictive coding

Shows that tPC can reproduce key behavioral phenomena observed in human memory, strengthening its biological relevance
Suggests that tPC can develop context-dependent representations and generalize to unseen sequences, linking to cognitive map theories
